episode_title: Olle Zacharison: How BBC News is Shaping its AI Strategy for the Next Era of Journalism

codes.description: Framing AI work as fundamentally about journalism and public service rather than technology, using AI as a navigational tool within an emerging ecosystem rather than an end in itself.
excerpts: I don't work with AI. I work with news journalism and public service journalism in particular, and now of course we navigate in an emerging AI ecosystem.
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Organizing AI work into four thematic pillars (boost productivity, reformat content, augment journalism, innovate user experiences) to connect standalone projects, coordinate cross-departmentally, and form long-term partnerships.
excerpts: we try to organize our work around four strategic themes. So instead of just saying like, hey, we use AI, we try to do it in a more uh structured sense. And that's been really good because then we can connect the dots better between different Projects that we have going on.
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: AI translation identified as the most mature AI project at the BBC, with a functioning side-by-side interface, precise time-savings benchmarks, and boosted output in BBC Mundo, while always requiring human sub-editing.
excerpts: Translation is maybe our most uh kind of mature project in the sense that we've been working on on AI translation for two three years. So there you have an actual interface like a tool where you have kind of side by side Translation from the original, then the AI assisted version, and then of course you have to sub-edit and correct it before. Publishing.
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: AI language tools work significantly better when English is one of the languages involved; going between two smaller non-English languages is much more challenging, creating an uneven playing field.
excerpts: The common denominator is often that all languages work better with AI if. English is involved as one of the languages, but going from two smaller languages can be much more challenging.
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Using customized GPTs to automate instrumental text fields in newsletter production (subject lines, metadata) while preserving journalists' creative writing for lead paragraphs and analysis.
excerpts: With newsletters, it's not the kind of stuff where we want to replace the creative writing. We always have a kind of a lead paragraph or an intro from one of our journalists. And of course we want to keep that. in their tonality and with their sharp analysis. But there's a lot of kind of little instrumental things you have to do before publishing a newsletter.
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Using AI to reformat existing content across aspect ratios, platforms, and formats (video resizing, subtitle generation, design templates) to reduce duplication where the same topic is produced independently in multiple places.
excerpts: We can sometimes produce news video on exactly the same topic. in five different places of BC News, you know, without really kind of synchronizing so well because it's also hard.
How can we use AI-powered tools to kind of Reformat news content like resize it or propose our design templates or subtitles
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Converting text-based sports articles into short daily AI-generated podcasts using script generation and synthetic voice, creating a new audio format from existing written content.
excerpts: it's a new podcast that we recently launched in Sports. which is called My Club Daily. So for a lot of the football clubs here we produce maybe five, six, ten articles a day on the website. But then we're using that now AI to convert that into a podcast format. So every day you get actually now it's kind of on five of the Premier League clubs
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Providing journalists with AI tools to sift through vast datasets and find story angles, particularly for high-quality investigations that differentiate the BBC from reactive everyday news reporting.
excerpts: augment our journalism is basically to kind of give our reporters tools to produce better original journalism. That can be, for example, high qualitativ investigations into topics.

this type of journalism is even more important for us in an AI ecosystem because a lot of the the kind of more reactive news reporting, like of everyday events, like Trump said something in a press conference. That's never gonna be unique to the BBC
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Recognizing that centralized AI initiatives are easier to track and showcase, but the invisible personal adoption of AI by individual skilled journalists may be even more impactful.
excerpts: where do we create the most value with AI? Is it through kind of centralized initiatives and pilots that my team is leading Or is it kind of let's go for full-on kind of personal adoption of AI skills in the newsroom
this more kind of invisible usage of AI, for example, in the hands of a really skilled journalist, it's perhaps even more important
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Peer-to-peer learning between journalists is more effective at driving AI adoption than top-down training from AI leaders, because colleagues are perceived as more credible and relevant than 'AI evangelists.'
excerpts: it's the kind of peer-to-peer learning between journalists that can really accelerate the use. Because it's not enough if a person like me shows up on the screen and says this can be terrific You have to hear it from a colleague
that's how some people see a person like me, of course, in in the newsroom. You know, that now he's gonna try to persuade me to use something but doesn't say something to me in my my everyday work.
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Journalists operating at near-100% production capacity lack time to explore AI, leading to low adoption not from skepticism but from sheer lack of bandwidth to engage with innovation.
excerpts: people are in almost hundred percent production. People sit in production and produce what they're supposed to do at work.

people don't have that much time to actually work around innovation and exploring AI and if you don't have that Knowledge of what these models can do, then you don't feel safe with them, and then you don't use it.
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: BBC journalists' deep commitment to credibility and trustworthiness makes them cautious about AI adoption, not from technophobia but from professional responsibility to maintain editorial standards.
excerpts: for BBC journalists they always have the credibility and the the trustworthiness of our content at heart. So i i I think it's more than that actually, that people haven't been able to devote so much time to it. then actually that they are really skeptika
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: BBC research documenting how AI assistants hallucinate and make false attributions to the BBC brand, distorting credible journalism and undermining editorial integrity without the publisher's consent.
excerpts: we've put out like a big piece of research showing very clearly how AI assistance are like every day distorting our content and kind of hallucinating and doing false attributions to to our brands.
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Soliciting open-ended AI ideas from all staff surfaced 1,224 submissions including brilliantly specific use cases that strategy teams could never have conceived, because front-line staff know their own workflow pain points.
excerpts: 1224 ideas came in through that free text field, and some of those ideas were so brilliant. They were so brilliant that we could never think them up in a kind of strategy team because people know their workflows, right? They know the pain points of their everyday work
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Self-critical acknowledgment that AI leadership has been too poor at mapping out specific needs of individual people and teams, defaulting to generic top-down advice instead of targeted support.
excerpts: I think we've been too poor at really kind of mapping out the needs of individual people and individual teams.
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Defining the BBC's product as a credible news experience rather than an AI experience, deliberately not emulating general-purpose AI assistants but instead using AI to serve journalistic purpose.
excerpts: People don't come to the BBC News app or the website to get AI, right? You go there to get credible news like w what's happening or what's happening around this story. For us it's It's not about kind of trying to emulate what the AI assistants are doing.
for us it's about kind of how can we give people a better news experience, not an AI experience in a way.
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Early data from Nordic conversational news services showing that 80% of users press pre-suggested prompts rather than typing their own queries, challenging assumptions about how people interact with chat-based news.
excerpts: I've heard now from early insights coming out from Aftonbladet in Sweden that also launched a conversation on U service and also Swedish radio. 80% just press the pre-suggested questions instead of typing in their own news query.
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Ruthless prioritization principle: not every idea deserves to become a project, and the discipline to kill bad ideas early is as important as the creativity to generate them.
excerpts: Just because it's an idea doesn't mean it's a good idea. You know, it's like kill this idea in its sleep, basically.
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Media companies' chronic pattern of launching MVP products, failing to iterate on user journeys, and jumping to the next project rather than following through on refinement.
excerpts: most prominently maybe that we haven't followed the product through. We have launched a kind of a an MVP uh version of something, and then we have jumped on to the next. thing.
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Generative AI enabling three-hour prototypes of capabilities that previously required six months of tool-building, fundamentally changing the economics and speed of experimentation.
excerpts: We've been in fifteen twenty years of digital transformation, we've been obsessed with building things. So we have to build a tool for this, you know, and we've started to do that. And that has been then delayed. Whereas now some of the stuff that we can do with Gen AI is like basically you can go in and in three hours customize the GPT that can perform a task that you had to build a tool for and using in six months.
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Identifying clusters of similar AI use cases across different teams (especially World Service language teams) and coordinating them into scalable suites rather than running duplicate standalone pilots.
excerpts: if we see and when we can coordinate in a much better way that hey here's a cluster of things, here's a suite of AI support in our production tools, for example, that we can work on and that we can have a more long-term vision together with our product group then that can create much more value and scale
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: In large organizations, cross-functional collaboration is easy at the executive level but breaks down at the operational base of the pyramid, where teams sit far apart and speak different cultural languages.
excerpts: often it's easy at the top to say, let's work in a cross-organisational sense because you have a management team and they sit together. But then you have a pyramid, right? So often like at the bottom of the pyramid, those teams sit very far apart. They maybe don't even speak the same cultural language
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Problematic pattern where editorial teams develop ideas in isolation for weeks or months, then hand off specifications to product/tech teams who have different priorities and question user need.
excerpts: the newsroom is running with something or the editorial management is running with something. And then we try to figure out those needs or the use case and often we have a brilliant idea, we think, right? Then we kind of proceed with that and we work all weeks or months with it. You can even kind of work in the newsroom with something and then we come and try to write out a ticket to the product department
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Advocating for product, tech, and editorial teams to begin discovery together from the earliest stages of an idea, rather than the sequential handoff model that produces misalignment.
excerpts: what we're trying to do is to start the discovery more together in the earliest stages of the ideas because even if we can't kind of implement and build or scale every idea that's that's coming out, then we have a better chance of at least achieving something
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Acknowledging that effective cross-organizational AI project execution depends heavily on personal relationships, mutual understanding, and physical meetings -- not just formal processes.
excerpts: it's about personal relations And personal relations might seem like, oh, that's e improper. You know, it's like projects shouldn't build on kind of be built on personal relations. But execution of projects are often built on good Cross-organizational relations
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: The AI leader's role as a translator between editorial and technical cultures, bridging different professional languages, terminologies, and methodologies.
excerpts: I see myself as a little bit of a translator, translator between these different worlds, but also from the tech side. They need to try to understand the editorial side better.
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Using product-side templates and structured processes for AI planning rather than editorial-style word documents, as product teams have better-developed planning methodologies.
excerpts: one of the best things I think that I did at Swedish Radio was when we constructed a new AI plan a year ago. We used the template from the product side to do it. So instead of having our own way of writing up a strategy or a document, a word document and kind of sending it over to to them.
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Frank acknowledgment that AI return on investment is currently negative for most projects, requiring more investment than it returns while capabilities are being built up, with precise time-savings being the most measurable metric.
excerpts: the return on investment is at this stage, it's often a negative return on investment in the sense that it requires more investment than what you get back, right?
when we are now doing alt text like the text description for images, we can manifest exactly down to the second how much time we save
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Building a bespoke fine-tuned LLM ('Style Assist') trained on paired datasets of incoming articles and their edited/published BBC versions, to align local democracy reporter submissions with BBC editorial standards.
excerpts: the style assist basically allows us to take Copies of or articles written by local democracy reporters around the UK because the BBC funds 150 reporters. Who often sit with a commercial private newspaper and they say send in brilliant kind of local stories to the BBC. And they send in 60,000 stories a year. So it's a big kind of inflow of articles. But we only publish maybe twelve 15,000 of them a year because we have to manually kind of sub-edit them
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Political impartiality in local reporting, especially capturing nuanced undertext of political arguments and giving all sides fair representation, identified as a particularly challenging dimension for the fine-tuned BBC LLM.
excerpts: impartiality is at the core of everything we do. And sometimes political nuances in these articles Let's say there's a big kans infectious local political question that they are arguing about there. Sometimes there's a nuance in like the positions between different parties that can actually sometimes also be a little bit in the undertext of the content. And we have seen some challenges there
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Positioning the BBC's brand trust, direct traffic, massive audience, and broadcast infrastructure as irreplicable competitive advantages that AI startups and aggregators cannot emulate.
excerpts: We have the most trusted news brand in the whole world. It's impossible for for a competitor to copy or emulate.
we have to boost up our confidence, like self-confidence in this era
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Counterintuitively positioning traditional broadcast infrastructure (radio, TV) as a defense against digital misinformation, since these channels exist outside manipulable digital platforms.
excerpts: we control like also analog infrastructure of course with all the Will the traditional broadcast that can actually also be an antidote for misinformation? Because it's basically not digital platforms.
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Provocative observation that media industry executives are more easily disrupted by AI anxiety than news audiences are, suggesting leadership panic may be more destabilizing than actual audience behavior shifts.
excerpts: Media industry executives are more easy to disrupt than news audiences, which is a very good thing.
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Repeated insistence that human oversight must accompany every AI output, with editorial responsibility always resting with human journalists regardless of AI's role in the workflow.
excerpts: human oversight with everything like that is of course key for us. You know, we can never surrender our editorial responsibility.
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Strategic advice to form alliances with AI pioneers across the organization through open-ended conversations rather than project-specific negotiations, using inspiration as the fuel of progress.
excerpts: form alliances, basically, with these kind of AI pioneers that we have in other parts of the business. So then you get inspired, right? And inspiration is the kind of fuel of progress.
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Using AI to generate alt text for images to improve accessibility for screen reader users, with precise benchmarking of time savings between manual and AI-powered workflows.
excerpts: we can of course use it for tagging. We could use that also in the next uh instance to kind of reformat content.
producing alt text, like text descriptions for images that we know are very important for accessibility for people using screen readers.
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Personal use of AI as a language assistance tool for a non-native English speaker working in an English-language organization, including custom GPTs for formal communication and decoding BBC-specific jargon.
excerpts: I use AI like every five minutes basically for my written communication

I have a special one that actually I've been asked to kind of license out and that's called my British Lingo Decoder. So I've asked it then to explain colloquial terms to me or all these kind of weird acronyms that they use in in the UK
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: The BBC's public charter obligation to reach the entire population with quality information serving as a guiding principle for AI decisions, distinguishing it from commercially driven organizations.
excerpts: we have public charters from the state to to reach like the whole population with qualitative information and and content.
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

codes.description: Specific quantification of the editorial bottleneck: 60,000 local democracy reporter submissions per year but only 12,000-15,000 published due to manual sub-editing requirements, with the fine-tuned LLM aimed at closing this gap.
excerpts: they send in 60,000 stories a year. So it's a big kind of inflow of articles. But we only publish maybe twelve 15,000 of them a year because we have to manually kind of sub-edit them and also align them with BBC style
codes.speaker: Olle Zacharison

episode_summary: Olle Zacharison presents a methodical, strategically organized approach to AI at BBC News, structured around four pillars (productivity, reformatting, augmenting journalism, user experience innovation) that aim to connect disparate standalone projects into coherent thematic clusters. The conversation reveals that the primary adoption barrier at the BBC is not journalist skepticism but production pressure -- staff at near-100% capacity simply lack time to explore AI, compounded by AI leadership's own failure to map individual workflow needs. The BBC's most technically ambitious project, a fine-tuned LLM for editorial style alignment, illustrates both the promise of bespoke models (potentially unlocking 45,000 unpublished local democracy stories) and their limits (struggling with political impartiality nuances). Zacharison's most counterintuitive insight is that media executives are more easily disrupted by AI anxiety than actual news audiences, suggesting the industry's existential panic may be self-inflicted rather than audience-driven.
surprises: The primary barrier to AI adoption at the BBC is not skepticism but journalists being at near-100% production capacity with no time allocated for exploration.
80% of users in Nordic conversational news services press pre-suggested prompts rather than typing their own queries, challenging the 'conversational' paradigm.
Zacharison characterizes the AI ROI as explicitly negative at this stage, framing it honestly as capability-building investment rather than current returns.
Analog broadcast infrastructure is positioned as a misinformation antidote specifically because it exists outside manipulable digital platforms.

1,224 ideas from a free-text field in a staff survey included use cases 'so brilliant that we could never think them up in a strategy team,' revealing the untapped innovation potential of front-line staff.
The observation that 'media industry executives are more easy to disrupt than news audiences' inverts the standard narrative about AI threatening journalism's audience.
tensions: Centralized AI strategy vs. individual adoption: Strategic thematic pillars provide organizational coherence, but the most impactful AI use may be the invisible personal adoption by individual journalists that is harder to direct or measure.

Brand protection vs. platform presence: The BBC blocks AI assistants from accessing content due to documented distortion, while simultaneously needing to be discoverable and present on the very platforms doing the distorting.

Fine-tuned LLM promise vs. impartiality limits: The Style Assist model works well for formatting and style but stumbles on the political impartiality that is the BBC's core editorial value, exposing limits precisely where stakes are highest.

Ruthless prioritization vs. bottom-up innovation: The 'kill ideas in their sleep' philosophy is in tension with the 1,224-idea goldmine from staff surveys, requiring judgment about which bottom-up ideas deserve to live.

Public service editorial responsibility vs. automation pressure: The 60,000-to-15,000 publication bottleneck creates strong incentive to automate, but the BBC's public charter demands that every published piece meets rigorous human editorial standards.

Speed of GenAI prototyping vs. organizational follow-through: Three-hour GPT customizations can be created rapidly, but the chronic MVP abandonment pattern means the organization may still fail to iterate them into mature products.
